item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was awesome… until the forced 'feels' at the end. What I had before the say-too-much hallucinations was perfect… then the author felt the need to spell it all out and add forced feeling-pull to it.
To me, that killed the punch, completly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cried, I actually cried while I was reading this. I was moved to tears from the emotions that I felt reading this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"From the distant glow we came" Oh. My. Gosh. This was awesome!

"on scroll-work wings of flame, to seek a truth in existence." Somehow less oomphy to me, but still awesome!

"Mom… Dad… I just want to go home!" *bawls like a baby for like an hour* Once this hit me fully, I was crying for quite some time!

This is bleedin' awesome! A refreshing difference from the dangerous norm of Foundation stuff! Upvoting when I figure out how!

I have an idea for a Tale based on this last guy. Can I do it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only problem is that the foundation did not seem to interview the scp-2500-b's (and if they did, they did not do so sufficiently). The foundation also made no attempt to remove the b's while in contact with the orbs, as that would almost certainly force the orbs or the b subjects to aknowlege the prescence of others.

Other wise it was a very good skip

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just so sad. +1

item.title: Re: All or some?
item.content:encoded:
B's probably account for a very small percentage of the population. My impression is that they're visiting aliens who have either possessed or recreated a human body for the purpose of collecting sentiments and feelings.

It's pretty clear that B is the child of both visiting A's. The Consciouness can collect samples in all subtype cases, but the B's individual consciousness/self-awareness will cease to exist upon death. SCP-2500-B-1 sacrificed himself so that the Consciousness could collect a sample of the experience of death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was beautiful and somewhat sad. I loved the writing and conversations.

(The only part I didn't like was the containment procedures. It's just not possible or necessary to contan.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I especially love the ending speech, an exceptionally well-written piece, that reveals just enough and keeps hiding just enough. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got the idea pretty early on (an inkling from the description, fully from the first addendum)…the rest felt like being beaten over the head repeatedly, just in case I hadn't gotten it yet. Overall, it's ok, but it's a bit heavy-handed for my tastes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel the same way. The whole thing seemed a little disjointed, but the conversation between the spheres seemed very thought out. If the author of the SCP reads this, all I have to say is work on focusing your thoughts, but otherwise, great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the majority of reading I was leaning towards a no-vote. It seemed like one of those skips that is intellectually intriguing, but doesn't really come together well for me. But at the end, with that speech, it all clicked. Totally worth a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn. This is quite…emotional. I'm not quite sure I totally understand it, but have an upvote anyway.

item.title: All or some?
item.content:encoded:
The article says there have only been a few (comparatively) manifestations of the entities that have been recorded. My question is, is the low number of manifestations because they only appear in particularly traumatic situations, or because there are only a few people who are part of the Consciousness?

In other words, are only some people part of the Consciousness? Or…is everyone actually part of It?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh gosh, I have a huge weakness for emotive extraterrestrial/extradimensional sentient SCPs. Heck I cry at Batteries Not Included.

I think the thing I appreciated the most was the syntax difference between the "parents" and the "children" - seems to echo the space we may never bridge between our own parents or our own children.

Also I enjoyed the idea that the human experience, that human suffering, is valued to such a degree by entities we'll never understand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to like this, but it really didn't go anywhere for me. I figured out after a couple lines of the first interview what was going on, and the rest of the piece didn't really add much to it. It's well written, to be sure, but it's monotonous. The final speech is way too speech-y for what it's supposed to be. It's all very pretty, but I can only admire it, not enjoy it. I'm sorry, but I have to downvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, but it takes an awful long time to get to it. The first interview delivers, but the dialogue was pretty "standard aliens", same with the second. I don't know, I think the execution's just off. This doesn't leave much of an impression on me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is markedly improved from the last draft I read… but I'm still not sure what I think of it. The concept is still 'meh', but the execution is pretty good, and the tweaks help a lot. Definitely wouldn't downvote, but have to think further on whether to upvote.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one. This should be the one.

+1

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: This is inappropriate. Please do not make posts like this in the future. Consider this an official warning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted long and hard like a horse's dick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is touching. Reminds me of SCP-370.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a typo- this is the French Military title, not the English. As for the B-167, it is certainly possible for the Foundation to capture someone after he was talking to the -As without knowing exactly who he is, and as stated, B-167 has been entirely uncooperative when it comes to questioning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a small typo… Sergent -> Sergeant

It's the interviews that make this piece, it is as if the humans were puppets. You know, this reminded me of a Book by ND Walsh, Conversations with God, which said that God wants to experience the world through people, which is a vibe that I am getting from this piece. Either it's a god or an alien entity that sends something in certain people to feel, represented as Consciousness; the principle is what counts.
From the end of the second interview, if we were to think about this religiously, are the lights actually warning the guy from being unable to go to heaven? Could be. And I gotta say, I really wanted to know more here.

Also

contained instance
identity unknown

Isn't this like an oxymoron? The Foundation contains people and don't know their identity…?

The lyrical end didn't do much for me, but I already had a picture. I really like the underlying implications going on here and so I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's fine, really. Not everything appeals to everyone, and I still appreciate people taking the time to comment even if they don't have concrete feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like this, but I can't figure out why. I think the concept is fine, even if I'm not super crazy about it. But something about how it's presented just… I don't know. Something here just doesn't work for me. Yeah, pretty useless feedback, I know. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was given pause by the fact that these things are having a fairly measured conversation with a dude who's just had his legs sawed off on a battlefield under enemy fire. I was also living in terror that I was going to get a "SURPRISE JESUS" moment somewhere in the article. In the end, the quiet beauty and genuine emotion of the interview logs convinced me to upvote. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 5ever and ever?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I figured. Just thought it'd be fun to ask.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chilling. The only thing i think is better than this is the apocalypse cradle. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it in the forums, still like it. Feels good knowing what I'm getting this go. Upvoteeeeeeeeeeee

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this doesn't win I will leave forever. No… I will leave 5ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No relation whatsoever. The Center in this context is merely the heart of what the entities are trying to build.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Question. The Center here. Any relation to another Center on this website?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, this is the one time I actually feel you on what a concept is delivering ;_; +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still imagine them to appear as the Shoes from Invader Zim.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The entities trying to reclaim the -B's early aren't doing it because they seek to accomplish anything, they're just worried parents, who act as such. There is no better outcome for the -Bs' efforts at recording experiences and emotions, it's just that those -B who we see are dispirited due to the hardship of the experience. They're still accomplishing what they came here to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt like this was a good dose of high-concept weirdness that, refreshingly, actually gives you enough information to understand what's going on. The containment procedures are sort of useless, but they serve as a means to tell the story. I am a little vague as to what the entities are trying to accomplish by reclaiming these individuals "early," as well as what the "better" outcome would have been if they hadn't "failed" at their task of… recording emotions? Whatever. The concept is vague and floaty, but at least it's there, and the story is told with passion. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ;_; It's beautiful. I want to cradle and comfort all of it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The work process for this SCP has been something of a strange one. The subject matter itself is fairly far from what I'm used to, and the dialogue has probably been edited more than any other SCP I've worked on. Still, when it's all said and done, I think I'm happy with the result. I hope you enjoy it.
